unit: Unit 9 Refrigerant and Oil Chemistry and Management
section: Section 2 Safety Tools and Equipment and Shop Practices

slides.title: Refrigerants and the Environment
bullets: Chemicals are reaching the earth’s atmosphere
Some of these chemicals are refrigerants
Refrigerants are stable if contained
Refrigerants are pollutants when released to the atmosphere

slides.title: Ozone Depletion
bullets: Ozone is found in the stratosphere and the troposphere
Stratospheric ozone shields the earth from excessive UV rays
Tropospheric ozone is considered a pollutant (smog)

slides.title: Atmospheric Regions
bullets: Figure 9.1 Atmospheric regions

slides.title: Effects of Ozone Depletion
bullets: Increase in skin cancer and cataracts in humans and animals
Adverse effects on plants and marine life
An ozone molecule is made up of three oxygen atoms
Chlorine destroys ozone molecules
One chlorine atom can destroy up to 100,000 ozone molecules

slides.title: Ozone Depletion Potential ODP
bullets: Higher ODP means more damage to stratospheric ozone
UV rays break off chlorine from CFC molecules
Chlorine attaches to ozone forming chlorine monoxide and oxygen

slides.title: An Ozone Molecule
bullets: Figure 9.2 A ozone molecule O3 and B standard oxygen molecule O2

slides.title: Breaking Up an Ozone Molecule
bullets: Figure 9.3 The breaking up of an ozone molecule by ultraviolet radiation

slides.title: Global Warming
bullets: Refrigerant gases prevent earth’s infrared radiation from escaping
Also called the greenhouse effect
HFC-134a has zero ODP but contributes to global warming
Total Equivalent Warming Impact TEWI measures global warming effects

slides.title: Refrigerants
bullets: Most refrigerants originate from methane and ethane molecules
Removing hydrogen and adding halogens creates halogenated refrigerants
Examples: CFCs, HCFCs, HFCs, HCs, HFOs

slides.title: Molecules in Refrigerants
bullets: Figure 9.6 Most refrigerants originate from methane and ethane
Both molecules are hydrocarbons

slides.title: CFC Refrigerants
bullets: Contain chlorine, fluorine, and carbon
Most damaging to stratospheric ozone because molecules persist
On July 1, 1992 it became illegal to intentionally vent CFCs
CFCs were phased out of production at the end of 1995

slides.title: HCFC Refrigerants
bullets: Contain hydrogen, chlorine, fluorine, and carbon
HCFC group scheduled for total phase-out by 2030
R-22 no longer used in new equipment after 2010; total phase-out in 2020
On July 1, 1992 it became illegal to intentionally vent HCFCs

slides.title: HFC Refrigerants
bullets: Contain hydrogen, fluorine, and carbon
Do not contain chlorine and will not deplete stratospheric ozone
Can contribute to global warming
On November 15, 1995 it became illegal to intentionally vent HFCs
R-12 systems can be retrofitted to operate with R-134a

slides.title: HFO Refrigerants
bullets: Contain hydrogen, fluorine, and carbon
Fourth generation refrigerants; environmentally friendly
Cost effective and energy efficient
Examples: HFO-1234yf and HFO-1234ze
Low global warming potentials GWP

slides.title: HC Refrigerants
bullets: Contain only hydrogen and carbon; no fluorine or chlorine
Zero ODP rating but still contribute to global warming
Highly flammable; limited use and limited charge amounts in USA
Common HCs: methane, ethane, propane, butane

slides.title: Flammable Refrigerant Applications Allowed
bullets: EPA SNAP approved examples: R-600a isobutane, R-290 propane, R-170 ethane
Also HFC-32 difluoromethane and R-441A HC blend

slides.title: Nameplates and Labels
bullets: Nameplate or serial label indicates what HC refrigerant can be used
Warning labels on evaporator sections, compressor compartment, exterior, exposed tubing

slides.title: Service Access and Tube Painting
bullets: Refrigerant piping for service must be colored red PMS #185
Red piping must extend at least 1 in from compressor and 1 in on each side of process tubes or service ports

slides.title: Compressor Terminal Venting
bullets: Occurs when terminal pins blow out of ceramic fusite terminal
Allows pressurized refrigerant and oil spray to escape
Spray can ignite from electrical sparking causing flash fires and severe injury
Terminal pins can be damaged by misuse or physical jolt

slides.title: Boiling Points and Safety Classifications
bullets: Hydrocarbon refrigerants have low boiling points
Liquid refrigerant exposure can cause severe frostbite
Eye exposure can cause blindness
Follow safety precautions for handling, transporting, and storing refrigerants and cylinders

slides.title: Refrigerant Numbering
bullets: Refer to refrigerants by number derived from chemical composition
Numbering system developed by DuPont

slides.title: Refrigerant Blends
bullets: Blends can contain up to five refrigerants
Azeotropic mixtures are blends of two or more liquids
Near-azeotropic mixtures can separate and fractionate when leaking
Zeotropic blends have greater glide and fractionation than near-azeotropic blends

slides.title: R Numbers for the Blends
bullets: 400-series blends are near-azeotropic
500-series blends are azeotropic
Blends may be named by percentage composition and may include capital letters

slides.title: Popular Refrigerants and Compatible Oils
bullets: HFC-134a R-134a is the alternative to CFC-12
HFCs make R-134a insoluble with mineral oils; require synthetic POE or PAG lubricants

slides.title: Refrigerant Oils and Their Applications
bullets: Many new oils exist; follow compressor manufacturer recommendations

slides.title: Oil Groups
bullets: Naphthenic oils commonly used in refrigeration
Popular synthetics: alkylbenzenes for HCFCs, glycols PAGs for automotive, esters POE for HFCs

slides.title: Regulations
bullets: Montreal Protocol limits use and production of CFCs and set phase-out dates
United States Clean Air Act of 1990 regulates use and disposal of CFCs and HCFCs
EPA enforces regulations

slides.title: Recover Recycle or Reclaim
bullets: Recovery removes refrigerant in any condition and stores it externally
Recycling cleans refrigerant by oil separation and passes through devices
Reclaiming processes refrigerant to new specifications

slides.title: Methods of Recovery
bullets: Passive or system-dependent recovery uses internal system pressure or compressor
Active recovery uses a self-contained recovery unit

slides.title: Technician Certification
bullets: Technician certification required since November 1995
Type I Small Appliance, Type II Medium/High/Very High Pressure, Type III Low Pressure
Universal Certification covers Types I II and III

slides.title: Mechanical Recovery Systems Methods
bullets: Fastest method: remove refrigerant in liquid state
Slowest method: remove in vapor phase
Recovery cylinders approved by DOT and should not be filled more than 80% liquid capacity

slides.title: Mechanical Recovery Systems Tips
bullets: Test refrigerants for acids before recharging
Avoid cross-contamination
Evacuate recovery cylinders to at least 1,000 microns before recovery
Weigh cylinder before and after recovery
Follow manufacturer instructions for recovery equipment

slides.title: Recovering Refrigerant From Small Appliances
bullets: Small appliances defined as hermetically sealed systems with 5 lbs or less of refrigerant
Recovery via low-loss fittings, Schrader valves, piercing valves
Refrigerant bags available for small appliance recovery as required by EPA

slides.title: Reclaiming Refrigerant
bullets: Return refrigerant to a reclamation center
Reclaim companies accept cylinders 100 lb and larger
Only DOT-approved cylinders with proper labels can be used
Refrigerant in reclaim cylinders must be of one type; mixed refrigerants cannot be separated

slides.title: Refrigerant Retrofitting
bullets: R-22 retrofitting considerations
New systems manufactured with R-410A
Best retrofit depends on temperatures, application, and equipment size

slides.title: General Retrofitting Considerations
bullets: Consider compatibility of plastics and elastomers
Lubricant selection and system performance issues
Most replacements are not pound-for-pound
Warranties may be impacted by retrofitting

slides.title: General Retrofitting Procedure Part 1
bullets: Identify and resolve existing adverse conditions
Obtain baseline data
Remove existing compressor oil if required
Recover existing refrigerant
Replace filter driers
Pressurize system with inert gas

slides.title: General Retrofitting Procedure Part 2
bullets: Evacuate the system then wait
Charge the system per manufacturer guidelines
Adjust thermostatic expansion valve and refrigerant flow controls
Record operating parameters
Label the system for future service personnel
